title: What Is a Grimoire?
intro: Before you write a single word, let's talk about what you're actually making. A Grimoire is not a notebook, not a journal, not a to-do list — it's a living record of your magical self. It holds your beliefs, your workings, your wisdom, and it grows as you grow.
instruction: On the first page of your book — or at the top of a new document if you're working digitally — write the word GRIMOIRE in whatever way feels right. Big, small, decorated, plain. Below it, write today's date and your name. That's all. You've begun.
reflection: What made you want to start this? What are you hoping this book will become for you?

title: Making It Yours
intro: A Grimoire that looks like someone else's is a beautiful thing to admire but a strange thing to use. Yours should feel like you when you open it — like coming home. Today we give it a personality.
instruction: Spend some time with your book or document and ask: what does it need to feel like mine? If you're working in a physical book, you might decorate the cover or inside cover with anything you have — a sticker, a drawing, handwriting in colours you love, a printed image you've taped in. If digital, choose fonts, colours, a background. There are no rules. Write or draw something that makes you smile when you see it.
reflection: If your Grimoire had a personality — a mood, a scent, a sound — what would it be?

title: Your Intention
intro: Every magical working benefits from a clear intention, and this book is a magical working. Not a rigid contract — just a warm declaration of what you're stepping into. Think of it as the spell you cast on the book itself.
instruction: Create a page titled 'My Intention for This Grimoire.' Write freely — a few lines or a full paragraph — about why you're creating this, what you want it to hold, how you want to feel when you use it. You might begin with 'I create this Grimoire to…' or whatever feels natural. No need to be perfect. This is between you and the book.
reflection: What kind of witch do you want this Grimoire to witness you becoming?

title: Your Symbol or Signature
intro: Many witches have a mark — a sigil, a symbol, an initial rendered in a particular way — that they use to sign their work and claim their space. Today you create yours, even if it's just a sketch, even if it changes later. This mark says: I made this.
instruction: On a fresh page, play with creating a personal symbol or magical signature. You might weave your initials together, draw a simple sigil based on your name, or invent something entirely. Sketch freely — it doesn't need to be polished. When one version feels right, draw it a little more deliberately and write underneath it: 'This is my mark.' Use it to sign your Grimoire pages going forward if you wish.
reflection: What do you want your mark to communicate about who you are?

title: Your Path
intro: Witchcraft is not one thing. It's a vast, sprawling territory with a thousand different roads through it, and yours is your own. Today you describe the path you walk — whether it has a name or not, whether it's tidy or wonderfully tangled.
instruction: Create a page called 'My Path.' Write about what draws you to the craft — what called you here, what resonates, what doesn't interest you. You might note if you identify with a particular tradition (hedge witch, kitchen witch, eclectic, folk magic, etc.) or you might simply describe the feeling of it: what it looks like, what it values, what it asks of you. There's no wrong answer.
reflection: When did you first know that magic was real for you — or that you wanted it to be?

title: What You Believe
intro: Magic works differently for everyone, and your beliefs shape your practice as surely as your tools do. There's room here for uncertainty, for questions, for 'I don't know but I feel…' That kind of honesty is more powerful than borrowed certainty.
instruction: Create a page called 'What I Believe.' Write about your relationship with the divine — whether that's gods, goddesses, spirits, the universe, nature, your own deeper self, or something you don't have a word for yet. Write about how you think magic works. Write about what feels sacred. You don't have to be certain. Write what's true right now.
reflection: Is there a belief you hold that you've never written down or said out loud before?

title: Your Magical Name
intro: Some witches have a magical name — a name they carry in their practice that holds something their everyday name doesn't. Some don't, and that's entirely valid too. Today is for exploring the idea, whether or not you choose to claim one.
instruction: Create a page called 'My Magical Name' — or, if you prefer, 'Names and Words That Call to Me.' If you have a magical name, write it here and explain how it came to you, what it means, why it fits. If you don't have one, write about names, words, or sounds that have always moved you — place names, old words, names from mythology or nature. See if anything wants to step forward. You're not committing to anything today.
reflection: If you could be called anything at all, what would it be — and what would that name say about who you are?

title: Your Tools
intro: You don't need a single special thing to practice magic — but most of us gather items that help us feel focused, connected, and intentional. Today you take stock of what you already have, even if it's just a candle stub and a notebook. Everything counts.
instruction: Create a page called 'My Tools.' List or describe the objects you use or feel drawn to in your practice — candles, crystals, cards, herbs, a particular cup, a piece of cloth, a feather, a stone from the garden. For each one, note what you use it for or why it matters to you. If you feel you have nothing yet, write about what you're drawn to and why — that's a tool list in the making.
reflection: Is there one object you own — magical or not — that always makes you feel more yourself? What is it?

title: Your Altar
intro: An altar is simply a space you've set apart as sacred — a shelf, a windowsill, a corner of a table, even a small tray you can put away between uses. It doesn't have to be elaborate or permanent. It just has to be yours.
instruction: Create a page called 'My Altar.' Describe your altar as it is now — what's on it, where it lives, how it's arranged. If you don't have a physical altar yet, describe the altar you would create if you could: what would be on it, where would it be, what would it feel like to stand in front of it? Draw a rough sketch if you like. Write about what you want your altar to hold for you.
reflection: What does it mean to you to have a space that is set aside as sacred, even if it's very small?

title: What Feels Sacred
intro: Beyond specific tools and altars, there are things that simply feel sacred to you — experiences, sensations, moments. The smell of rain. A particular piece of music. A view from your window. These belong in your Grimoire just as much as any spell.
instruction: Create a page called 'What Feels Sacred to Me.' Write freely — a list, a paragraph, fragments — about the things that make you feel connected to something larger than yourself. These might be grand or very small. Include sensory things: a scent, a texture, a sound, a taste, a colour. Don't edit or second-guess. If it makes you feel it, write it down.
reflection: When was the last time you felt a sense of the sacred? What were you doing, where were you, what did it feel like?

title: Spells and Workings You've Tried
intro: Every witch has a history, even if it's brief or informal. The wishes you made at candles before you called it magic. The times you felt something shift. The workings you tried from a book or invented yourself. Today you honour that history.
instruction: Create a page called 'Workings I Have Done.' Write about spells, rituals, or magical acts you've tried — whether they worked, partly worked, or seemed to do nothing. Note what you used, what you intended, and what happened. If you're brand new and haven't tried anything yet, write about a magical act you want to try and what draws you to it. This page will grow over time.
reflection: Is there a moment in your life — before or after you called yourself a witch — that you now recognise as magic?

title: Earth
intro: Earth is the element of the body, of roots, of patience and physical things. It's what holds you up when you sit or lie down right now. It's bones and soil and stone and the slow, quiet steadiness of things that last.
instruction: Create a page for the element of Earth. Write about what Earth means to you — its qualities, its gifts, its challenges. Note what Earth feels like in your body (heaviness, stillness, groundedness). Write about your relationship with this element: are you naturally earthy, or does it feel foreign? List any symbols, colours, creatures, plants, or objects you associate with Earth. You might draw something too — a stone, a tree, a simple pattern.
reflection: In what areas of your life do you feel most grounded — and where do you feel the least?

title: Air
intro: Air is the element of thought, of communication, of the breath you are breathing right now without thinking about it. It's ideas arriving without invitation, the wind that carries seeds, the voice that says something true.
instruction: Create a page for the element of Air. Write about what Air means to you — its qualities, its gifts, its restlessness. Notice how your breath moves as you write. Write about your relationship with this element: are you a natural thinker, a talker, a curious mind always reaching? Or does Air feel difficult — scattered, anxious? List symbols, colours, creatures, sounds, or sensations you associate with Air.
reflection: What thought keeps returning to you lately — the one you haven't quite let yourself examine?

title: Fire
intro: Fire is the element of will, of passion, of transformation that is sometimes uncomfortable. A flame changes everything it touches. Fire is also warmth, the hearth, the light in the dark — protection as much as power.
instruction: Create a page for the element of Fire. Write about what Fire means to you — its energy, its danger, its gift of illumination and transformation. Write about your relationship with this element: where does your fire live? What lights you up and what burns you out? List symbols, colours, creatures, feelings, or images you associate with Fire. If you have a candle, you might light it while you write.
reflection: What have you been holding back — in your craft, your life, your sense of yourself — that might benefit from a little fire?

title: Water
intro: Water is the element of emotion, of intuition, of the tides within you that pull and recede. It's dreams and depth, the cup you hold, the tears you've cried, the way feelings move through you like weather.
instruction: Create a page for the element of Water. Write about what Water means to you — its depth, its flow, its capacity for both nurture and overwhelm. Write about your relationship with this element: are you naturally intuitive and emotional, or do feelings feel confusing? List symbols, colours, creatures, tastes, textures, or memories you associate with Water. Note any bodies of water — real or imagined — that feel significant to you.
reflection: What emotion are you currently letting yourself fully feel — and what emotion are you keeping at arm's length?

title: The Moon
intro: The moon is one of the great companions of the witch — cycling, reliable, affecting the tides and the blood and the light in the night sky. You don't need to be outdoors to feel her. She's there whether you look or not.
instruction: Create a page called 'The Moon.' Write about the moon phases and what they mean to you — new, waxing, full, waning, dark. Note which phase you feel most aligned with right now, and which you find most challenging. Write about any moon rituals you practice or want to practice. Look up today's moon phase and record it. This page can be added to over time as you work with the moon.
reflection: How does your energy, mood, or body change across the lunar cycle? What patterns have you noticed?

title: The Wheel of the Year
intro: The Wheel of the Year is the cycle of eight sabbats — the turning points of the seasons that many witches mark and celebrate. You don't have to observe all of them, and there's no wrong way to honour them. But knowing the wheel gives you a map.
instruction: Create a page called 'The Wheel of the Year.' List the eight sabbats — Samhain, Yule, Imbolc, Ostara, Beltane, Litha, Lughnasadh, Mabon — and write a few words about what each one means to you or calls up in you. Note which ones you already observe, which you're curious about, and which you feel less connection to. This is a living page — return to it each sabbat and add what you noticed.
reflection: Which season or turning point of the year feels most magical to you, and why do you think that is?

title: Herbs and Plants
intro: The plant world has been in conversation with healers and witches for as long as there have been witches to listen. You don't need a garden or a cabinet full of dried herbs. You can begin with what you know, what you remember, or what you can see from where you sit.
instruction: Create a page called 'My Herbs and Plants.' List any herbs, plants, or trees that feel significant to you — whether you grow them, cook with them, buy them as tea, remember from childhood, or simply love the idea of. For each one, note what it means to you or what you use it for, even if that's just 'I love the smell' or 'grandmother grew it.' This page will grow slowly and that's perfect.
reflection: Is there a plant — a flower, a tree, a herb — that you've always felt drawn to? What is it, and what do you think it's trying to tell you?

title: The Natural World You Know
intro: You don't have to be outdoors to have a relationship with the natural world. The sky through your window is still the sky. The birds you hear are still birds. The weather that changes everything is still changing everything. Today you record the nature that reaches you.
instruction: Create a page called 'The Natural World I Know.' Write about nature as you experience it from your life: the view from your window, the plants in your home, the creatures you hear or see, the weather patterns that affect you, the seasons as they come to you. If you have memories of places in nature that were important to you, record them here too. This is your ecology — unique and valid.
reflection: What does the natural world ask of you — and what do you wish you could offer it in return?

title: Your Magical Correspondences
intro: Correspondences are the relationships between things — this colour for that intention, this herb for this feeling, this moon phase for that kind of working. Over time you build your own map of them, based on tradition but also on what simply works for you.
instruction: Create a page called 'My Correspondences.' Begin building your personal reference: pick five to ten things you're drawn to — a colour, a herb, a number, a stone, a day of the week, an animal — and write down what you associate each one with magically or emotionally. Don't rely on a reference book for this; write from instinct first. You can always add traditional meanings later. Your felt sense is the foundation.
reflection: Is there a colour, number, or symbol that seems to follow you through your life? What might it be trying to say?

title: Dreams
intro: Dreams are one of the oldest forms of magical communication — messages from your deeper self, from whatever you believe lies beneath ordinary consciousness. You don't need to be a master dream interpreter. You just need to start paying attention.
instruction: Create a page called 'Dreams.' Write about your relationship with dreaming: do you remember your dreams easily, rarely, or not at all? Record any recent dream that stayed with you, even just fragments. If you can't remember a recent dream, write about a recurring dream from any point in your life, or a dream that has never left you. Begin keeping a very brief dream log — even one line in the morning counts.
reflection: What is the most significant dream you've ever had — the one you still think about? What do you believe it was telling you?

title: Symbols That Find You
intro: Symbols don't always arrive through books or deliberate study. Sometimes they just appear — in a recurring image, a shape you keep drawing without thinking, a creature you seem to encounter everywhere. These uninvited symbols are often the most personal.
instruction: Create a page called 'Symbols and Signs.' Write about symbols, images, or shapes that recur in your life, your dreams, or your imagination. This might be an animal you keep seeing, a number that follows you, a particular image that appears when you close your eyes, a shape you doodle without thinking. For each one, write what it might mean to you — not what a book says, but what you feel.
reflection: If something in the world has been trying to get your attention lately — a repeated sighting, an image, a coincidence — what is it, and have you been listening?

title: Your Animal Allies
intro: Many witches feel a particular kinship with certain animals — not necessarily as formal spirit guides, but as creatures they return to again and again in thought, in dreams, in art. These connections matter and belong in your Grimoire.
instruction: Create a page called 'Animal Allies.' Write about the animals you feel most connected to — real creatures, mythic creatures, or both. For each one, describe the connection: is it a feeling of kinship, a recurring dream presence, an animal you've always loved, or something stranger and harder to name? Write about what each animal teaches you or represents for you. You don't need to justify any of this.
reflection: Is there an animal you've always loved in a way that felt like more than preference — like recognition? What is it about them?

title: Meeting Your Shadow
intro: Shadow work is the practice of looking gently at the parts of yourself you've hidden, minimised, or disowned — not to punish yourself for having them, but to understand yourself more fully. We begin this slowly. There is no rush and no requirement to go further than feels right.
instruction: Create a page called 'Shadow Work — A Beginning.' Write about one trait or pattern in yourself that you find difficult to admit or look at. You don't have to understand it fully — just name it, as honestly as you can. Then write one sentence that extends compassion to that part of you: something like 'I see you and I'm not afraid of you' or whatever words feel true. That's enough for today.
reflection: Is there something about yourself you've been told — or told yourself — is unacceptable? Where did that message come from?

title: What You Carry
intro: All of us carry things — old stories, inherited fears, wounds that haven't fully healed, beliefs that were given to us before we were old enough to choose them. The Grimoire is a place to acknowledge what you carry without being defined by it.
instruction: Create a page called 'What I Carry.' Write — as honestly as you can manage today — about one story, belief, or wound you carry that affects your practice or your sense of yourself as a witch. This might be: a belief that you're not really magical, a fear of being judged, a family story about women and power, an old shame. Write it down. Then write: 'I am more than this story.' Return to this page when you're ready to write more.
reflection: If you could lay down one thing you've been carrying — just for today — what would you choose, and what would it feel like to set it down?

title: Reclaiming
intro: Shadow work isn't only about looking at the difficult — it's also about reclaiming what was taken, dismissed, or buried. The parts of yourself that were told to be quiet. Today we give them a little air.
instruction: Create a page called 'What I Reclaim.' Write about a quality, gift, or aspect of yourself that you were taught to hide or downplay — your intensity, your sensitivity, your strangeness, your power, your voice. Write about what that quality actually is and what it's capable of. Write a short declaration reclaiming it — something like 'I reclaim my…' in words that feel true and fierce and yours.
reflection: What would change in your practice — or your life — if you allowed yourself to be fully, unapologetically yourself?

title: Sealing Your Grimoire
intro: You have built something real. Twenty-six pages of yourself — your beliefs, your tools, your elements, your shadows, your reclamations. Today we seal this book with intention, not to close it, but to honour what it has already become.
instruction: Create a page called 'The Sealing.' Write a short closing statement for this version of your Grimoire — a few sentences or a paragraph that acknowledges what you've done and dedicates the book. You might include your magical mark from Day 4. You might write the date. You might light a candle as you write. Write something like: 'I seal this Grimoire with my full intention. It holds my truth as it is today.' Make it yours.
reflection: What surprised you about this process? What do you know about yourself now that you didn't know — or wouldn't admit — when you began?

title: The Promise to Return
intro: A Grimoire is never finished. It grows as you grow, and there will be pages you want to rewrite, sections you want to add, things you couldn't say yet that will become sayable with time. Today you make the promise that keeps the book alive.
instruction: Create a final page called 'The Promise.' Write a commitment to yourself and to your practice — not a rigid vow, but a warm intention. Something like: 'I promise to return to these pages, to add what I learn, to let this book change as I change.' Write the next three pages or sections you want to add to this Grimoire when you're ready. Then close the book — physically, or close the document — and hold what you've made for a moment.
reflection: The witch who opened this Grimoire on Day One and the witch closing it today — what is the difference between them?
